title: Satya Nadella ơn A.I. Jobs: Humans Will Do the ‘Glue Work’

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: Let's get started with the show. Our first guest tonight is someone we've been very excited, we've been trying to get him on the show for many years. He finally agreed and he's here tonight. Everyone please give a warm welcome to Satya Nadella, CEO of Microsoft.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: Now Satya, I have to start by making a confession, which is that I have not been a regular user of a Microsoft AI product since 2023 when I when one of them tried to break up my marriage. Unsuccessfully I should add, my wife is right there.

full_transcript.speaker: Satya Nadella
full_transcript.text: Those were the days, right? Didn't have think about a world without guardrails.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: But so much has been happening at Microsoft with AI since then. So just catch us up like what for people who may not have tuned in a little while, what have you guys been up to in AI?

full_transcript.speaker: Satya Nadella
full_transcript.text: Yeah, I mean, look, the fundamental thing that I feel we're about to move from is not talking about AI as a one thing, to sort of having even a mental picture of what is an ecosystem that is sort of driven by AI, right? So today if you think about it, even since when you first used Sydney to now, it has been about a frontier model you've sort of talked about fable, what have you. But if we are ever going to transition to an economy that is driven by AI, it can't be about one model, it can't be about three firms, it has to be something that's broadly felt where the economy is at the frontier, not a firm or a model is at the frontier. So Microsoft as a platform company, to me that's what we are up to, right? So to me we had our developer conference last week, it was all about, hey, can we build the platform and the tools where every enterprise in every country can operate at the frontier. To me that's the question, to be saying, hey, my model does this, but the economy is growing at 2% means this is not going to end well, unless we sort of really get to a place where the economy is inflecting in terms of its economic growth and it's widespread because the frontier benefits are. That's what happened in electricity and every other technology which was a general purpose technology.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: Let me ask about one of the platform shifts that you signaled at build last week when you announced Project Solara. You said it would be agent-first hardware, kind of next-generation set of devices that would have agents running them. Tell us a little bit more about that, what that looks like. Can you give us an example of maybe what a Project...

full_transcript.speaker: Satya Nadella
full_transcript.text: Yeah, there were two things Casey we did at Build which were interesting, right? One was we took the PC itself, in fact Jensen had done it the previous night at Computex where he talked about, you know, if the the pick...

full_transcript.speaker: Satya Nadella
full_transcript.text: Sure, I wanted... one of my favorite pictures was Jensen with the desktops, the laptops, because the... and he obviously had the RTX chip, which is a new SOC, with essentially a petaflop of compute right on the PC. So that was about new functionality coming to the old form factor, right? So think about what I describe as unmetered intelligence, right? So the fact that you can have a Windows computer that can run a one you know a trillion-parameter model locally, I think is going to be very needed if we're going to ever have tokens, I mean models, agents running 24 by 7. But then the question it sets up is, in a world where we have these models and these agents that are long-running, can I have like a badge? Uh, right? If I'm a nurse in a hospital and I'm walking to from station to station, can I just take out this device which can scan, which can input, which can take my speech output and turn it into a prompt? Uh, that is what I think an agent-first device looks like, right? So it's sort of really a new, instead the centrality of the phone, I think, is still going to be there for a lot of apps that we use, but in an agent world, you kind of have that ambient intelligence that is like a sense of feel that then works with your models. And so that's what goal is, to invent the new form factors that are not beholden to the old form factors for this functionality.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: It's super interesting, and I want to hear a lot more about it. We have many more questions about AI, but I wanted to ask about one more device that has been in the news today, which is the Xbox. The leaders of the Xbox division put out a memo today saying that we should expect a hard reset of Xbox coming soon. They said that there are massive increases in the prices of components and that Xbox might need a new business model. So as a big gamer who's enjoyed many happy hours on Xbox, Satya, I have to ask: what is your strategy for Xbox?

full_transcript.speaker: Satya Nadella
full_transcript.text: Yeah. So look, in fact, we're, you know, this is the 25th year of Xbox, and we're very thrilled about the progress we've made. I mean, gaming in an interesting way at Microsoft is older than even Windows and Office, right? The first app we built was the Flight Simulator. And so it's got a long heritage. Xbox itself has been there for 24, 25 years. The challenge now for us is to think about how do you innovate both in hardware as well as in the games going forward in a world, in an economically viable way, right? I think one of the things that Sarah, who has just taken over Xbox, put out is that we've invested a lot. No one can accuse Microsoft of not having invested for the last 25 years. And now we have to turn this into a sustainable business.

full_transcript.speaker: Satya Nadella
full_transcript.text: That delivers what is fundamentally one of the best sources of entertainment still. The challenge we have is we've not been monetizing that entertainment. In fact, if anything, we've been subsidizing that entertainment. In fact, like there's more monetization of Xbox games happening on YouTube than at Microsoft. And so that doesn't mean we go do things that are unnatural. We want us to do what is really our job, which is to build great games, build great hardware, but we've got to do it in an economically sustainable way. So I think Asha's really 100 days in and she put out a post saying in the next 100 days, she's going to take a fresh look and make sure we deliver on what our fans expect of us, both on the hardware side or on the publishing side.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: I mean, can you give us just any more detail? Like, when I hear that, I think, okay, so maybe like the Xbox gets way more expensive, the games get way more expensive? Like, is there any sort of like carrot you can offer the gamers?

full_transcript.speaker: Satya Nadella
full_transcript.text: I think we have to find ways to deliver the games in a way which it's economically relevant for the customer and for us. So today, there's a issue, in fact, unfortunately, because of what's happening with the cloud and AI, the prices have gone up, right? It's happening with PCs, it's happening with phones, Xbox is impacted as well. So the scarcity of the semiconductor supply and memory in particular are having a massive impact on consumer electronics all up. That's a temporal thing. That, I think, we'll get through. It is not going to be a permanent. But there is a permanent thing, which is what's the Xbox model going forward? And that's where if you think about it, like, PCs and consoles both have their place, obviously mobile has, people play and elsewhere, and so we have to now bring it all together while staying true to what we've always done.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: Satya, I want to take you back in a time machine. The year is 2023. The board of OpenAI has just fired Sam Altman, one of Microsoft's biggest partners. You and your team spend a hurried weekend trying to pull together an entirely new division of Microsoft, Microsoft Advanced AI Research, to sort of catch the employees that are making a mass exodus from OpenAI. You're ordering laptops, you're opening up an office in San Francisco so that all these people have a place to go work. The company looks like it's on the verge of collapse. And then nothing happens. Then, then Sam gets rehired and OpenAI stands back up on its feet. Um, and I want to present you, as part of this time machine experiment, with a piece of rare merchandise, which I recently acquired from an OpenAI employee, which is a Microsoft Advanced AI Research...

full_transcript.speaker: Satya Nadella
full_transcript.text: All right. That is awesome!

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: sweatshirt. To be clear, this division never existed, and I was told by the person who made this that they had to sort of fudge a little bit on the sort of copying and printing shop application, which made them prove that they were a Microsoft employee to get...

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: But this is for you, if you ever want to take that walk down memory lane.

full_transcript.speaker: Satya Nadella
full_transcript.text: Thank you. Thank you for this. Oh man, that weekend. I'll remember that forever, and thank you for this, but all I remember, quite frankly, of the weekend is India getting thrashed by Australia in cricket. That was the more tragic thing.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: So if that had happened, in the world where all of these OpenAI employees ended up working at Microsoft in a new advanced AI research division run by Sam Altman and Greg Brockman, in that world, is Microsoft better or worse off with AI than it is today in this world?

full_transcript.speaker: Satya Nadella
full_transcript.text: Look, we're thrilled that Greg and Sam made it back to OpenAI and they are where they are and they've now, as you said, filed an S-1 or what have you. Look, it's fascinating, right? When we initially took the bet on OpenAI, it was a research lab, a non-profit entity that had created a for-profit unit, and said, 'Hey,' they went and shopped around and said, 'Who can back our crazy idea that intelligence is log of compute?' And quite frankly, there were lots of people that were at Microsoft at that time who thought this is nuts. But, you know, we said, 'I think this is a worthwhile thing to back,' and quite frankly, we changed, I think, and they changed through their work and OpenAI through their work, the world. And here we are in 2024, and we're thrilled about it.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: You guys recently renegotiated your deal between OpenAI and Microsoft. And I understand what OpenAI got out of that deal. They got the ability to work with multiple cloud providers, to be a little bit more open about how they commercialize their technology. What did Microsoft get out of the revision of that deal?

full_transcript.speaker: Satya Nadella
full_transcript.text: I mean, we have a lot of, you know, interests in OpenAI. We're obviously on their cap table and we are a customer of theirs, a large one. We are their source of IP for us, all the way to GPT-4, and so all of the things. And at the same time, we have the ability and the flexibility to reuse the IP, build our own IP. We just last week launched MAI models, which we've built from the ground up. We published in fact the paper which I think should help people even get the capability we have. Especially if you take those two thoughts, right? Which is: here is intelligence is log of compute and here is a pure lineage model from Microsoft that climbed all the way, means that we now have the ability to keep going. And to us, and our infrastructure, I must mention that we wouldn't have been where we are with even Azure but for that close partnership with OpenAI. So we have the compute, we have now the model, and we have still the partnership.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: Let's talk about those models. Is your goal to make a frontier best model in the world? And if so, what's the strategy for overtaking a ChatGPT, a Gemini, a Claude?

full_transcript.speaker: Satya Nadella
full_transcript.text: Yeah, so I think the way I would say it is our real goal is to get everyone across the ecosystem to the frontier. So we're going to take a slightly different take in saying, for example, if you think about what's—how does one build a frontier model? You hill climb, you RL, and then you need data, right? So at this point, we have saturated the data. And so that means you're basically hoovering the data from every place, right? So the question is, what if you turn that around and said, no, there's a base model that has reasoning, that has the agent loop, but you can bring it into your RL. Every company, right? If you—if the future of the firm is human capital and token capital, I want every balance sheet, every income statement in every company to have both. And that's our goal with our frontier model. Our model should be the best model that they can use as a base and keep even the weights, definitely the harness and the context, which is theirs, and they can replace our model with anything else. So that to me is more of a vision that I think is what Micro—I always ask the question, why does Microsoft or why does the world need Microsoft? And if we're successful, can the world around us be successful? This I believe is a more sustainable way to go at it.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: We have to ask a question about the AI backlash that we're seeing around the country. Graduation speakers are getting booed. AI is polling terribly. Lots of people upset about data centers. What role does Microsoft have in that image or helping to solve that? And how do you think the industry can find a path forward that involved, I don't know, being a little more popular?

full_transcript.speaker: Satya Nadella
full_transcript.text: Yeah. Yeah. I think we can start quite frankly by painting a picture and delivering the results on why there are more than, you know, everyone is a stakeholder, right? You can't go out there and say I have this unbelievable technology, except you're not going to have a job and in fact we're going to take all your water and all your energy and, you know, good luck. I mean, that cannot be an—and no wonder there is so much anxiety, right? You talked about the students or you can talk about a community. And so therefore you've got to do the hard work at this point. It is what it is, right? So you can't deny that the perception is terrible. And so I feel at Microsoft, we want—whether it's like, take the data centers. It's fascinating. We've been operating in Quincy, Washington for 20 plus years and I just, you know, we celebrated our 20th year. You know, if I look at what all has happened in Quincy in the 20 years, their tax base has gone up, their taxes locally have gone down, they have more employment locally...

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: Because of the data center.

full_transcript.speaker: Satya Nadella
full_transcript.text: Because of the data centers.

full_transcript.speaker: Satya Nadella
full_transcript.text: Data center. In fact, the data center, it's kind of like basically it's become a data center town. And when we did this, you know, cookout and people came and they celebrated the rejuvenation of Quincy, Washington, because of our presence for the last 20 years. That's the first longitudinal thing of 20 years that I've seen. And that's what the communities where data centers are, they definitely can't increase price on energy, they have to be very, you know, in fact, they should replenish all the water they use and create economic opportunity. So that's on the data center side. But across the economy, if there are small businesses feeling like, wow, AI is making me more productive, if every large multinational is able to say, oh, I'm building that token capital and human capital, right, because the big question is employment. Everybody thinks that all jobs are going away. But if you sort of, and I'm not saying there won't be real displacement, the workflow doesn't change, but take software development. Of course, you know, software development is now all agentic. Except if you think about even the evolution of the GitHub app, right, you know, when I had 100 CLIs, what did I need? I need a new IDE. It's called an ADE, right? A back end, again, some piece of software that helps me manage all of this complexity. So I think we have to think about new work that gets done, which will be metacognition, metawork, that is going to have wages and we have to be concrete about that.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: But help us understand like what your own view is of the potential disruption, right? Like because I've been talking to so many economists, tech leaders like yourself about this over the past couple of months, and truly opinion is all over the place. And I talk to some folks who say, yes, you better believe I'm hiring fewer people next year because of AI. And then I talk to other people who say, I can't get enough engineers, I want more than I have. So where are you on that? In two years, are you going to have more engineers or fewer?

full_transcript.speaker: Satya Nadella
full_transcript.text: Yeah, so I think the if in the early 80s if someone had come to us and said, hey, we're going to have three and a half billion people in the world who are all going to be typists, we would have said why does the world need three and a half billion typists? Except we do. We all get up in the morning and type, but we're doing quote-unquote information work, knowledge work, and so on. So that type of change is going to have to happen and each of them will have a name and it'll have a wage support. That reinvention, right? So a software developer of the past to the software developer of the future may have the similar sort of skills, but the work they do is different because they're managing a group of 100 agents, a thousand agents. In fact, there's a beautiful term one of my colleagues has, which is just like in software development we always had this concept of test coverage, one of the new things that we are learning is what I'll call—

full_transcript.speaker: Satya Nadella
full_transcript.text: ...or cognitive coverage, right? So what does a software developer do? I have a repo full of code that was written by agents, I am cognitively understanding what happened, and I now need tools for cognitive coverage on what that has built. That's a job, I think, of a software developer. And in order to do that, you've got to go to school, you've got to learn computer science and have cognitive coverage. And so this reinvention of work, the work artifact, the workflow, right? Because software went from input to output. That's a format change, an artifact change, the workflow is changed, and the work changes with it.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: I feel like what people really want to hear is some combination of like, your job isn't going to change that much, or if it does change, you're going to get paid more. Do you think that either of those things will be true for most people?

full_transcript.speaker: Satya Nadella
full_transcript.text: I think that that's the thing. The wages, you know, have always been about what is it that we as a society value? Right? I mean, we've grown—the last 200 years, 250 years was about a particular form of expertise and a pool of knowledge. So when you have abundance of some form of expertise, what is that human ability to now build a new expertise that is not trainable? Right? In fact, there was a nice blog I read this morning from Sarah Guo, which I thought was an interesting one, where she sort of mentions, hey, what is the untrainable part? And that applies to organizations and I think us as well. And it's—we as humans have agency, ambition, that should not be counted out. Uh, and if you look at even what is human capital today, the human capital in spite of all the digital systems we have at our disposal, we do the glue work. We will discover the new glue work that happens with all this automation, and that I think is the process of change.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: One idea that's been floated recently, including by, uh, reportedly Sam Altman, and President Trump has also weighed in on it, is the idea of having the US government take direct investment stakes in frontier AI companies. Do you think that's a good idea? What percent of Microsoft would you like the US government to own?

full_transcript.speaker: Satya Nadella
full_transcript.text: MSFT, you can trade.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: But do you think that is a way for the gains of AI to be more broadly felt?

full_transcript.speaker: Satya Nadella
full_transcript.text: You know, look, this is all very new, right? I mean, the idea that there may be the United States, whether it has a sovereign fund and the sovereign fund has equity stakes, and that somehow, uh, is part of what is considered the wealth of the citizens of this country, I think it's a novel idea. Other countries have done it. I think Alaska has some form of it in the state because of the oil wealth. Uh, so I'm not sort of opposed to innovative ideas like this, uh, but at the end of the day, I mean, there's this entire movement of if only we had invested some portion of our social security in S&P 500, we would have a surplus or what have you. And so to the degree to which some of these ideas can...

full_transcript.speaker: Satya Nadella
full_transcript.text: played out and and they succeed, I think we'll all benefit from it.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: You um told Dwarkesh Patel in February 2025 that your benchmark for achieving AGI was 10% GDP growth. Uh seems like we're not super close to hitting 10% yet. Um I'm curious like how you view that kind of statement you made a year ago now and do you see any sort of recent acceleration that makes you think it's more possible?

full_transcript.speaker: Satya Nadella
full_transcript.text: In fact, you know, it's one of the things that I think a lot about is the difficulty of even a very powerful general purpose technology and its diffusion and the amount of change management that is required, right? I mean, the systems, so for example, one of the challenges right now that we're going to face in the next year, two years, is this, you know, the economics of tokens. Uh for example, the hard truth is that the marginal cost of productivity improvement has to match the marginal cost of the token. That's a management discipline, right? So you can't just say, hey, I love token maxing because it's sort of money in my bank. The business has to benefit from it. And that is what is going to really drive it. So in fact, it's fascinating. The equation for that 10% growth would be when you have a perfect match between the marginal cost of the token to the marginal value and it's priced, right? So that means it's the best way to get at it.

full_transcript.speaker: Satya Nadella
full_transcript.text: If that happens, 10% is definitely going to happen. But not definitely what's happening right now where everybody goes and writes codes and token maxes, that's not a way to achieve 10% growth.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: How much uh sort of token maxing has been going on at Microsoft in the last year?

full_transcript.speaker: Satya Nadella
full_transcript.text: A lot.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Satya Nadella
full_transcript.text: And I'm out of it. And what I mean by that is um you know, look, I want people to obviously and myself, I'm like a token maxer too, so the it is addictive, it's kind of like, hey, I love this thing. So then you have to step back when the novelty wears off to say, what is it that I'm trying to create? Uh in fact, the thing that I love now in in Co-pilot now is our auto-mode. And so we now have a very good, we have an economic model that's feeding it as well. Basically, I say don't use frontier models for non-frontier problems, right? Please let's kind of match these things such that you get the outputs, you get the economics, uh and it's not it can't be a race to just doing things that just don't add value.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: Give us a flavor of like Satya's token maxing, like what are some of your your big token projects lately?

full_transcript.speaker: Satya Nadella
full_transcript.text: Yeah, I mean like the one thing that I recently um built was, you know, I've always sort of felt that I want a repo that is in sync all the time with discussions that are happening out of band, uh that I'm not in.

full_transcript.speaker: Satya Nadella
full_transcript.text: Right, think about even that concept. See, I like that thing because it sort of is sort of not possible today, right? So you have essentially the ability now to have an agent that literally is looking at all the work discussions that may be related to your repo and creating the plan and executing the plan. And it's just, in fact, all I did was I put Work IQ, which is the database underneath all of Microsoft 365, as an MCP server to my coding agent, and I said keep watching that, and every time people discuss this repo, please change my repo. And it keeps working. And so this is the best way to keep your basically your model, you know, in sync with all requirements that ever come up.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: You've been thinking a lot about the political economy of AI, obviously, you know, the things you're talking about about diffusion and adoption and GDP growth are all part of that. What do you think the people in San Francisco leading the AI companies here get wrong about the political economy of AI?

full_transcript.speaker: Satya Nadella
full_transcript.text: I mean, I wouldn't say they're wrong about the political economy of AI, but I do think when I look back at the—there's a very core book I read I think in December. It's called—I may have the name wrong, but it's written by Joel Mokyr and a couple of co-authors on—I think it's called Parallel Paths to Prosperity. They describe a little bit of how the last thousand years the United—you know, the West grew and what was happening in China. So it was a thousand-year history. But the fundamental thing when I take from that book and in general when I read history is, the West in particular got three things into a virtuous cycle, right? They got technological revolutions, markets, and democracy. All both acting as a check on the other. That's why there is no such thing as an economy. It's a political economy. A democracy controls ultimately what happens in a market, and then technology sort of tries to disrupt the two, and then you keep sort of the checks and balances. That's magical. It's one of the most unbelievable social constructs ever to emerge in the world, right? Think about sort of, you know, it became the model. And we now need that same model to be redefined for this age. But it will work because it worked the last time. And so therefore, I think us reminding ourselves that the balance, the checks that each one has on the other, is what I think we have to aspire for, whether it's in San Francisco, whether it's in Washington, D.C., or quite frankly, anywhere else.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: Maybe just as a last question, I'm still trying to like hone in on what I think Kevin might call how AGI-pilled you are. Like, there's a sense in the—in Silicon Valley that it really is different this time, and that the jagged frontier is going to keep advancing forever, and all of a sudden the little tasks that AI can automate today are going to convert into full jobs. How much do you buy that story?

full_transcript.speaker: Satya Nadella
full_transcript.text: Look, I- I buy that anything where the loops can be closed, right, like coding. In fact, AI research is sort of possible to close. I think we have now got sufficient, I'd say, evidence of that. But is that enough? I don't think so. Um, and- and when I think about, you know, people talk about how verifiable is this task, and in the messy real world of even knowledge work, just saying I'm going to look at the traces of human activity is enough to close the loop? I don't think so. That I think is the- the challenge, which is when I am in a meeting, I say things, I note things, I may observe things, but what I do with it is not a trace today that I can RL my way in, right? And that to me is where we're selling short what is, I would say, unverifiable part of the human capital. And so to me, that's where- the... so I believe the advances keep happening. I still am in the more in the world of, hey, this is platforms, tools, very powerful, very disruptive. I have a lot of, sort of, you know, humility to say a lot of things will change, but at the end of the day, so was electricity, so were a lot of other, you know, steam when it first came out and what have you. And so I'm not sort of sitting there and thinking this is the last technology we ever will invent. I don't buy that. I- I kind of feel like, yeah, this is in the pantheon of all technologies a big step up, but I do think that, you know, there will be more to come.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: Very good. Well, Satya Nadella, thanks so much for joining us. Please give Satya a hand.

full_transcript.speaker: Satya Nadella
full_transcript.text: Thank you.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: Thank you so much.

full_transcript.speaker: Satya Nadella
full_transcript.text: Thank you so much. Thank you.

highlights: Satya Nadella emphasizes that for AI to truly transform the economy, it must move beyond a competition between a few "frontier models" to become a broad platform that empowers every enterprise and country to operate at the frontier.

Microsoft is shifting focus toward "agent-first" hardware and form factors that provide ambient, unmetered intelligence, envisioning a future where AI agents run 24/7 on devices that move beyond the traditional centrality of the smartphone.

Nadella suggests that achieving massive GDP growth through AI requires a management discipline he calls matching the "marginal cost of the token" to the marginal value it creates, rather than simply using high-powered models for non-essential tasks.

Regarding the future of work, Nadella argues that humans will transition into roles focused on "cognitive coverage"—managing and verifying the outputs of complex AI agent networks—while continuing to provide the essential "glue work" driven by human agency and ambition.

Addressing the future of Xbox, Nadella indicates a strategic pivot toward economic sustainability, moving away from subsidizing entertainment toward a business model that better monetizes high-quality games and hardware.
summary:
In this interview, Microsoft CEO Satya Nadella outlines a strategic shift from treating AI as a singular product to viewing it as a broad, platform-driven ecosystem. He emphasizes that for AI to truly transform the economy, it must function as a general-purpose technology—similar to electricity—where every enterprise can operate at the "frontier." To support this, Microsoft is moving toward "agent-first" hardware and "unmetered intelligence," envisioning a future where local, high-compute models run continuously on personal devices. Nadella also addresses the evolving business of gaming, noting that the Xbox division is undergoing a "hard reset" to find a more sustainable business model amid rising hardware costs and shifting monetization trends.

Regarding Microsoft’s competitive landscape, Nadella reflects on the 2023 OpenAI leadership crisis, reaffirming the strength of the partnership while highlighting Microsoft’s move to develop its own in-house frontier models (such as MAI). He remains grounded regarding the AI backlash and fears of job displacement, arguing that while AI will disrupt workflows, it will ultimately lead to a "reinvention of work." In this new era, he suggests that human value will shift toward "cognitive coverage"—the ability to manage and verify the work of hundreds of AI agents. Ultimately, Nadella views AI as a massive evolutionary step in technology rather than the "last invention," asserting that human agency and ambition remain the essential "glue" that automation cannot replace.
description:
"Everyone is a stakeholder" in A.I. Satya Nadella, the chief executive of Microsoft, said during a live event in San Francisco with Hard Fork, The New York Times's tech podcast.

In a wide-ranging conversation with Kevin Roose and Casey Newton, the hosts of Hard Fork, Satya Nadella addressed the A.I. economy, the backlash against A.I. across the United States, as well as President Trump’s recent comments that Americans could get rich by sharing in the wealth of A.I. companies.
 
Nadella also shared what he's maxing out his A.I. tokens on, why he's skeptical that software developers will ever fully be replaced, and his plan to create a new business model for Xbox.

0:01 Intro
0:55 Latest on Microsoft and A.I.
2:28 Project Solara
4:36 XBox’s new business model?
7:53 Kevin’s gift to Satya
9:18 Microsoft and OpenAI relationship
11:49 Can Microsoft make the best A.I. model in the world?
13:24 A.I. and data center backlash
15:45 Job disruption
19:47 Should the government invest in A.I. companies?
21:05 Are we close to AGI?
22:39 Tokenmaxxing at Microsoft
24:44 Political economy of A.I.
26:40 How AGI-pilled are you, Satya?

Additional Reading
Microsoft C.E.O. Satya Nadella Says, ‘Everyone Is a Stakeholder’ in A.I. https://www.nytimes.com/2026/06/10/technology/microsoft-satya-nadella-artificial-intelligence.html

Hard Fork is a weekly look into the future that's already here. Hosts Kevin Roose and Casey Newton explore stories from the bleeding edge of tech.

“Hard Fork” is hosted by Kevin Roose and Casey Newton. Produced by Rachel Cohn and Whitney Jones. We’re edited by Vjeran Pavic. Engineering by Chris Wood and original music by Dan Powell, Marion Lozano, Sophia Lanman and Rowan Niemisto. Fact-checking by Caitlin Love. Video production by Jake Nicol, Chris Schodt and Sawyer Roque. Podcast Art Direction by Benjamin Wilkerson Tousley. Podcast Art & Animations by Julian Hespenheide. Additional Motion graphics by Phil Robibero. Thumbnails by Julia Moburg and Tara Godvin. Special thanks to Paula Szuchman, Pui-Wing Tam and Dahlia Haddad.
topics: AI's impact on the global economy and Microsoft's platform strategy
The evolving partnership and relationship between Microsoft and OpenAI
Future business models and hardware strategies for Xbox gaming
Job disruption and the changing nature of work for software developers
The political economy of AI and societal backlash against data centers
